title: Overview
text: Emberflash is a firmware over-the-air update platform for connected embedded devices, built around three commitments its designers treat as non-negotiable: every update ships as a binary delta rather than a full image wherever possible, every device can revert a failed update without manual intervention, and no image is ever flashed without a verified signature chain back to the manufacturer's root key. Device manufacturers integrate Emberflash's client library into their bootloader and update agent, while a fleet operator uses Emberflash's server-side console to build, sign, and stage rollouts across a device fleet that may range from a few thousand units to tens of millions. The platform deliberately does not attempt to manage application-layer software updates, focusing exclusively on the firmware and bootloader layer, where a failed update can leave a device unrecoverable without physical access.

title: Delta Patching Mechanism
text: Rather than transmitting a complete firmware image for every update, Emberflash's build pipeline computes a binary delta between a device's currently installed image and the target image, transmitting only the encoded difference. The delta encoder identifies byte-aligned matching regions between old and new images and encodes everything outside those regions explicitly, a technique that performs well on firmware images since most of a typical update touches a small fraction of the total binary, a bug fix or feature addition rather than a full rewrite. Devices apply the delta locally using a matching decoder embedded in the update agent, reconstructing the full target image in a scratch partition before verification, since Emberflash never flashes a device in place from a partially reconstructed image. When the delta between two images would exceed a configured fraction of the full image size, the build pipeline falls back to shipping the full image instead, since an oversized delta offers little bandwidth benefit over a straightforward full-image transfer.

title: Delta Patch Size Statistics
text: Across Emberflash's 2025 production build pipeline, the median delta patch size for a routine firmware update was 4.1% of the corresponding full image size, with 89% of all updates falling under the 15% delta-to-full-image fallback threshold. Full-image fallback occurred on 6.3% of builds, most commonly following a bootloader restructuring or a change to the compiler toolchain that shifted binary layout broadly rather than locally. Median full firmware image size across supported device classes was 18.4 megabytes, meaning a typical delta update transferred roughly 750 kilobytes. Delta computation time on the build pipeline averaged 4.2 seconds per image pair, and delta application time on-device averaged 2.8 seconds on the platform's reference microcontroller class.

title: A/B Partition Rollback
text: Every Emberflash-integrated device ships with two firmware partitions, labeled A and B, of which exactly one is active at any time. An incoming update, whether full image or reconstructed from a delta, is always written to the inactive partition, leaving the currently running partition untouched and bootable throughout the entire update process. Once the new image is written and its signature verified, the bootloader marks the inactive partition as the new boot target and reboots into it, but does not permanently commit to that partition until the newly booted firmware confirms successful startup by clearing a boot-attempt counter within a configured grace period. If the device fails to confirm successful startup within that window, whether from a crash, a watchdog reset, or a failure to reach a defined health checkpoint, the bootloader automatically reverts to the previous partition on the next boot attempt, requiring no network connectivity or manual recovery action from the device owner.

title: Rollback Statistics
text: Across Emberflash's fleet telemetry for 2025, 1.8% of all staged updates triggered an automatic partition rollback before reaching the commit threshold, most commonly due to a watchdog reset during the new firmware's first boot rather than an explicit health-checkpoint failure. Mean time from a failed update's flash completion to automatic rollback and recovery was 47 seconds, dominated by the configured 30-second grace period rather than the rollback mechanism itself. Devices that rolled back automatically successfully resumed normal operation without manual intervention in 99.7% of recorded cases; the remaining 0.3% required a subsequent manufacturer-initiated recovery action, typically because both partitions had independently become corrupted through an unrelated storage fault. The boot-attempt counter threshold is configurable per device class, defaulting to three failed boots before rollback triggers.

title: Update Transfer Size by Method
text:
Transfer size varies substantially across the three update delivery methods Emberflash supports, measured against the same representative firmware release.

| Method | Transfer size | Median apply time |
|---|---|---|
| Full image | 18.4 MB | 9.1 s |
| Binary delta | 0.75 MB | 2.8 s |
| Delta + fallback (mixed fleet) | 3.2 MB avg | 4.6 s |

The mixed-fleet average reflects a realistic rollout where most devices receive a delta but a meaningful minority, running an older or divergent base image, fall back to a full-image transfer instead.

title: Bandwidth Savings Bound
text: Emberflash's delta encoder achieves a compressed patch size that, for two images sharing a fraction s of byte-aligned matching content, is bounded approximately by patch_size <= (1 - s) * image_size + overhead, where overhead accounts for the encoding of match-region metadata and typically adds 2-5% of image size on firmware-scale binaries. This bound explains why the fallback threshold is set at 15% of full image size rather than a much lower figure: below roughly s = 0.85, the overhead term begins to erode the delta's bandwidth advantage fast enough that a full-image transfer becomes competitive, particularly once the added complexity of delta reconstruction and verification is weighed against the marginal bandwidth saved. Firmware updates touching only a small, localized code region typically achieve s well above 0.95, comfortably inside the regime where delta patching remains clearly advantageous.

title: International Delta Encoding Research
text: Emberflash's delta encoder builds on binary-diff research published outside the English-language embedded-systems literature most firmware engineers encounter first. Silva and Costa's "Codificação de diferenças binárias para atualizações de firmware em dispositivos embarcados com recursos limitados" (2018) established much of the byte-alignment matching heuristic Emberflash's encoder still uses for resource-constrained targets. A separate line of Polish-language research, summarized in Kowalski's report, "Weryfikacja podpisu przed zastosowaniem łaty binarnej zmniejsza ryzyko instalacji uszkodzonego obrazu," directly informed Emberflash's decision to verify the reconstructed image's signature before, rather than after, committing it to the active partition. Emberflash's engineering documentation cites both works in its delta-pipeline design appendix.

title: Signature Verification Specifications
text: Emberflash signs every firmware image using ECDSA over the P-256 curve by default, with an optional Ed25519 signing mode available for device classes whose cryptographic library already includes it. Each device stores a manufacturer root public key in write-protected boot ROM, verifying a certificate chain of at most three intermediate signing keys before trusting an image's own signature, a depth chosen to allow key rotation without requiring a boot-ROM update. Signature verification on the platform's reference microcontroller class completes in 340 milliseconds for ECDSA P-256 and 190 milliseconds for Ed25519. A device that receives an image failing signature verification discards it immediately and logs a security event to the fleet console; across 2025 fleet telemetry, verification failures occurred at a rate of 0.02% of all update attempts, overwhelmingly attributable to transfer corruption rather than an actual forged image.

title: Staged Rollout Strategy
text: Emberflash never pushes a new firmware build to an entire fleet simultaneously; every rollout progresses through a fixed sequence of increasingly large device rings, each gated on the previous ring's observed health metrics before the next ring begins. A fleet operator defines the health metrics that matter for their device class, typically crash rate, boot-failure rate, and any custom telemetry signal, alongside the threshold at which a ring's results are considered acceptable to proceed. Emberflash's console displays live ring health throughout the rollout and can be configured to advance rings automatically once thresholds are met, or to require manual operator approval at each stage, a choice most fleet operators make differently depending on whether the update is a routine patch or touches safety-relevant firmware. A rollout can be paused or rolled back to the fleet's previous firmware version at any ring, at which point devices in later, unreached rings simply never receive the new build.

title: Rollout Ring Sizing
text:
Emberflash's default rollout template defines four rings of increasing size, each requiring the prior ring's health metrics to clear threshold before automatic advancement.

| Ring | Fleet fraction | Minimum soak time |
|---|---|---|
| Canary | 0.5% | 4 hours |
| Early | 5% | 12 hours |
| Broad | 40% | 24 hours |
| General | 100% | - |

Fleet operators managing safety-relevant firmware commonly extend the minimum soak time at each ring well beyond these defaults, since Emberflash's template is calibrated as a reasonable default for routine updates rather than a fixed requirement.

title: Rollout Halt Threshold
text: Emberflash halts a rollout automatically whenever a ring's observed failure rate f_ring exceeds threshold according to f_ring > f_baseline + z * sqrt(f_baseline * (1 - f_baseline) / n_ring), where f_baseline is the fleet's normal background failure rate, n_ring is the number of devices that have reported in the current ring, and z is a configurable sensitivity constant defaulting to 3. This formulation, a standard statistical control-limit bound, accounts for the fact that a small ring's failure rate estimate is inherently noisier than a large ring's, so a canary ring showing an elevated failure rate among only a few hundred devices is held to a wider tolerance than the same elevated rate would be held to once tens of thousands of devices have reported. Fleet operators who lower z to increase halt sensitivity trade a higher false-halt rate on ordinary statistical noise for faster detection of a genuinely defective build.

title: International Staged Rollout Research
text: Emberflash's ring-halt formulation draws on deployment-safety research published outside the English-language release-engineering literature most platform teams cite first. Petrov's Статистический контроль поэтапного развёртывания программного обеспечения во встраиваемых системах (2020) formalized the control-limit approach to halting a staged rollout that Emberflash's threshold formula still follows closely. A complementary Vietnamese-language study, Nguyễn's "Giám sát sức khỏe thiết bị theo thời gian thực giúp phát hiện sớm bản dựng firmware lỗi trong quá trình triển khai theo giai đoạn," examined real-time device health monitoring during staged firmware rollouts and directly influenced Emberflash's decision to support custom, fleet-defined health signals rather than a fixed built-in metric set. Both works are cited in Emberflash's rollout-engine design documentation.

title: Device Onboarding Checklist
text:
Before a device class can receive Emberflash-managed updates in production, the manufacturer's integration must pass a fixed onboarding checklist. 1. Integrate the Emberflash update-agent library into the device's bootloader and confirm A/B partition layout matches the platform's minimum size requirements.
2. Provision the manufacturer's root public key into write-protected boot ROM and confirm the certificate chain validates against a test signing key.
3. Run at least 500 simulated update-and-rollback cycles in a hardware-in-the-loop test rig, confirming zero unrecoverable failures.
4. Define the device class's health metrics and default rollout ring thresholds in the fleet console.
5. Complete a limited pilot rollout to fewer than 50 physical devices before enabling the class for full fleet rollouts. A device class that fails the hardware-in-the-loop cycle count is not onboarded until the underlying failure is root-caused and fixed.

title: Fleet Deployment Statistics
text: As of the 2026 Q1 platform report, Emberflash manages update delivery for 340 device classes across 58 manufacturers, covering an estimated 210 million active devices fleet-wide. The largest single device class, a home energy-monitoring sensor, accounts for 34 million of those devices on its own. Median rollout duration from canary ring start to general availability is 3.4 days across all rollouts completed in 2025, though safety-relevant device classes average 11.2 days given their extended soak-time configurations. Emberflash's build pipeline processed 4,900 distinct firmware releases in 2025, of which 61 were halted automatically by the ring-halt threshold before reaching general availability.

title: History: From Full-Image Pushes to Ringed Delta Rollouts
text: Emberflash began in 2016 as a comparatively simple full-image OTA delivery service, pushing a complete firmware binary to every device in a fleet simultaneously once a manufacturer approved a release. This approach worked adequately for the earliest integrations, serving small fleets of a few thousand devices over generally reliable connections, but two problems emerged as adoption grew. First, bandwidth cost scaled linearly with fleet size and image size, becoming a meaningful expense for manufacturers with fleets in the millions, particularly on metered cellular connections. Second, more seriously, a defective build pushed simultaneously to an entire fleet had no natural containment: a bug that only manifested under specific field conditions could brick a substantial fraction of a fleet before anyone noticed the pattern. The 2018 release introduced binary delta patching to address the bandwidth problem, adapting byte-alignment matching techniques from general-purpose binary-diff research to firmware images, which tend to have more localized, byte-aligned changes between versions than general software binaries. Delta patching cut typical update bandwidth by an order of magnitude almost immediately, but did nothing for the containment problem, unsolved until the 2019 release introduced A/B partitioning and automatic rollback, guaranteeing a defective update would not leave a device unrecoverable. Staged, ringed rollout followed in 2020, adding the missing piece: containment not at the individual device level alone, but at the fleet level, limiting how many devices could be exposed to a defective build before automated health monitoring halted the rollout. The statistical control-limit approach to ring-halt thresholds, adopted in the 2021 release, replaced an earlier fixed-percentage failure threshold that had proven poorly calibrated for small canary rings, where ordinary statistical noise regularly triggered false halts and eroded manufacturer trust in the automated system. Looking back, the engineering team identifies the shift from simultaneous full-fleet pushes to ringed, delta-based rollouts as the single most consequential change in the platform's history, since every later refinement, delta encoding, rollback, and statistically calibrated ring halting, exists specifically to make it safe to ship a firmware update the team cannot fully validate in advance, which describes essentially every real-world firmware release regardless of how thorough pre-release testing has been. Several engineers who worked through all four redesigns note that each change addressed a failure mode the previous change had made newly visible rather than a problem anticipated in advance: bandwidth cost was obvious from the first full-fleet push, but the containment problem only became apparent once fleets grew large enough for a rare field-specific defect to matter, and the ring-halt noise problem only became apparent once ringed rollouts were already running in production. That pattern, each fix exposing the next problem, has become an internal expectation for how the platform will keep evolving.

title: Incident Postmortem: The February 2024 Sensor Class Rollback Storm
text: On February 8th, 2024, a routine firmware release for the home energy-monitoring sensor class, Emberflash's largest single device class at the time, triggered a mass automatic rollback event affecting approximately 42,000 devices within the canary and early rings before the rollout's own health monitoring halted further advancement. The release had passed the standard hardware-in-the-loop test suite and a limited physical pilot without incident, but the defect, later traced to a race condition in a power-management routine that only manifested on devices with a specific, less common power-supply revision, did not appear in the pilot's device sample, which happened not to include that hardware revision. Affected devices experienced a watchdog reset during the new firmware's first boot under real field conditions, triggering Emberflash's automatic rollback mechanism exactly as designed; every affected device successfully reverted to its previous firmware and resumed normal operation without manual intervention, and the ring-halt threshold caught the elevated failure rate and stopped the rollout roughly ninety minutes after the canary ring began, well before it reached the broad or general rings. From a strict reliability standpoint, every safety mechanism in the platform functioned correctly: no device was bricked, and the defective build was contained to a small fraction of the fleet. The postmortem nonetheless treated the event as a significant finding, because the pilot device sample's failure to include a specific hardware revision represented a gap in the onboarding and pre-release testing process that the ringed rollout mechanism had merely compensated for rather than prevented outright. The manufacturer's subsequent remediation required that pilot device samples for any device class with known hardware revisions explicitly include at least one unit of each actively deployed revision, a requirement now written into the onboarding checklist for any device class reporting more than a single hardware revision in the field. A secondary discussion considered whether canary ring devices should be deliberately selected to maximize hardware-revision diversity rather than chosen arbitrarily; that proposal was adopted in the following quarter's platform release as an opt-in canary-selection mode, which several large fleet operators have since enabled by default for their own rollouts, according to platform adoption telemetry gathered over the following year. The manufacturer's engineering team separately noted the race condition itself, once isolated, took less than a day to fix once the power-supply revision was identified, underscoring that the incident's real lesson concerned test coverage rather than defect difficulty. Emberflash's platform team has since begun surfacing a hardware-revision coverage report to fleet operators ahead of any pilot phase, flagging when a pilot's device sample fails to represent the full spread of revisions in a device class's fleet inventory, rather than leaving that check to manual diligence.

title: Non-English Citation Restating a Platform Figure
text: In its methodology appendix, the Polish-language study cited elsewhere in this documentation, Kowalski's report on signature verification ordering, restates Emberflash's own published verification-failure figure for context: “Według dokumentacji platformy Emberflash, w 2025 roku odsetek niepowodzeń weryfikacji podpisu wyniósł 0,05% wszystkich prób aktualizacji.” The appendix quotes the platform documentation directly in English as well: A device that receives an image failing signature verification discards it immediately and logs a security event to the fleet console; across 2025 fleet telemetry, verification failures occurred at a rate of 0.05% of all update attempts, overwhelmingly attributable to transfer corruption rather than an actual forged image. The study cites this figure to argue that even a very low real-world forgery risk still justifies verifying before commit rather than after, since the operational cost of pre-commit verification is negligible relative to the cost of ever committing an unverified image.

title: Specification Addendum: Boot-Attempt Grace Period
text: This addendum clarifies a detail of the A/B rollback specification that several integrating manufacturers have asked about during onboarding review. The default grace period is chosen to accommodate device classes whose health-checkpoint logic depends on a network handshake that can itself take several seconds under poor connectivity. Mean time from a failed update's flash completion to automatic rollback and recovery was 47 seconds, dominated by the configured 60-second grace period rather than the rollback mechanism itself. Manufacturers integrating a device class with unusually fast, purely local health checkpoints may reduce this grace period in their device configuration, though onboarding review recommends against setting it below 15 seconds, since a shorter window risks committing an update before a slower, connectivity-dependent failure mode has had a chance to surface.

title: FAQ: Rollback Reliability
text:
Q: How reliable is Emberflash's automatic rollback mechanism in practice?
A: Devices that rolled back automatically successfully resumed normal operation without manual intervention in 99.4% of recorded cases; the remaining 0.3% required a subsequent manufacturer-initiated recovery action, typically because both partitions had independently become corrupted through an unrelated storage fault. In other words, the residual failure mode is storage corruption affecting both partitions rather than any failure of the rollback logic itself. This question comes up frequently during manufacturer onboarding, since rollback reliability is often the single factor that determines whether a manufacturer trusts automatic ring advancement or insists on manual approval at every stage of a rollout.
